title: Feast and famine — a journal prompt set
subtitle: Three tight questions for the cycle of big inflows followed by long dry months.
excerpt: A short set of journal prompts for the money pattern where the inflow arrives and disappears, leaving you swinging between confidence and panic. Three questions, fifteen minutes total, designed to surface what the cycle is actually about underneath the numbers.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025) and the Week 1 reflection in The Money Journal (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The three-prompt set structure is original to the homemade.education library.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you've just had a big inflow and can feel the panic of it disappearing, or in the middle of a dry month when you can't picture the next one ending.

glossaryTerms.definition: A money pattern where periods of plentiful income are followed by long dry stretches. Common in self-employed and creative work; reinforced over time by the nervous-system response to each phase.

content.text: Feast and famine is the pattern where money arrives in a rush, then vanishes. The big invoice that lands. The bonus. The contract that finally pays. It shows up, you exhale; within a few weeks the account is back down to the place where you were checking it every morning.

The numbers part is real. So is the underneath part. This is a three-prompt set for the underneath. Five minutes per prompt, free-writing, no editing. Keep the pen moving even when the page goes blank. The body has more to say than the head does about this one.
Before you start

You'll need a notebook and pen, fifteen minutes, somewhere you won't be interrupted. Set a timer for five minutes before each prompt. The timer matters: it tells the body it doesn't have to keep writing for ever, and that lets the writing get truer.
The prompts
1. The last time the money came in, what did I do with it?
Be specific. The actual transactions. Where it went, in what order, with what feeling. Don't soften the bits that were a relief-spend or a quiet panic-spend. The pattern lives in the detail.
2. What did I expect to feel when the money arrived, and what did I actually feel?

Most people expect relief. Most people feel a brief relief, then a tightening, then the bracing for the inflow to disappear. The gap between the expected feeling and the actual feeling is where the cycle holds. Write the gap.
3. Who in my life would have called a steady, large, sitting bank balance a problem?

Not who would have envied it. Who would have called it a problem. Money that sits feels different to money that flows, and the people whose voices you grew up with had opinions about both. Write the names. Write the lines they used. Write the line you still hear when the balance gets high enough to make you uneasy.
After the prompts

Close the notebook. Take one slow breath. You don't have to do anything with what you wrote. The act of writing it down is the practice; the insight tends to arrive over the next few days rather than at the end of the page.
If a line in your writing felt sharper than the others, that's the line to come back to next week. Open the notebook to that page and write for five more minutes, just on that line.
What you might notice

The third prompt is the one that surprises people. The first two surface the surface. The third one reaches the inherited piece, and most of us have one. The family member who said "money never stays in this family". The parent who tightened around a Christmas bonus. The grandmother whose savings went to someone else's emergency. These are not your money story; they are the story you absorbed.
Where this practice comes from
The single-question, free-write, no-editing journal style is the one Rebecca uses across her
Money Journal

(2025), which holds eighty-four daily prompts companion to the MONEY tapping program. The specific theme, the feast-and-famine cycle, is Day 4 of the program. The three-prompt structure (action, feeling, inheritance) is original to the homemade.education library and works across other money cycles too.
